name: Kushinadahime
shortDescription: A princess of Izumo, saved from the Yamata no Orochi by Susanoo, who later became his wife.
description: Kushinadahime, also known as Kushiinadahime, is a prominent figure in Japanese mythology, primarily known for her role in the myth of Susanoo's slaying of the Yamata no Orochi, an eight-headed serpent. She was one of the eight daughters of the earthly deities Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi. Due to the Yamata no Orochi's annual consumption of one of their daughters, Kushinadahime was the last remaining. Upon encountering the grieving parents, the storm god Susanoo pledged to slay the serpent in exchange for Kushinadahime's hand in marriage. He transformed her into a comb, placing her in his hair for protection during the battle. After Susanoo successfully defeated the Yamata no Orochi, Kushinadahime reverted to her human form and became his wife. She is revered as a symbol of purity, sacrifice, and marital fidelity. Her story highlights the themes of protection, divine intervention, and the triumph over monstrous forces.
subtitle: The Comb-Princess Saved by the Storm God
role: Princess, wife of a god, symbol of purity and marital fidelity

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Kushinadahime's story is primarily detailed in the Kojiki, Japan's oldest chronicle. Born to Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi, she was the last of eight daughters destined to be devoured by the Yamata no Orochi. Susanoo, exiled from the heavens, encountered the grieving family and learned of their plight. He devised a plan to intoxicate the serpent with strong sake. During the serpent's drunken stupor, Susanoo slayed it, discovering the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi sword within its tail. He then restored Kushinadahime to her human form and married her. This act is seen as a pivotal moment in Susanoo's transformation from a rebellious deity to a protector of humanity.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Kushinadahime embodies the ideal of a virtuous and pure maiden in Japanese mythology. Her willingness to be sacrificed highlights the themes of duty and familial love. Her rescue by Susanoo and subsequent marriage to him reinforces the importance of divine intervention and the establishment of order over chaos. She is often invoked in prayers for marital happiness, safe childbirth, and protection from evil. Shrines dedicated to her and Susanoo are found throughout Japan, signifying their enduring presence in the religious landscape.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Kushinadahime's transformation into a comb symbolizes protection and concealment. The comb itself is a traditional Japanese hair ornament, often associated with femininity and beauty. Her vulnerability and dependence on Susanoo for rescue represent the reliance on divine power to overcome adversity. The slaying of the Yamata no Orochi can be interpreted as the triumph of good over evil, and the establishment of a harmonious relationship between the divine and human realms. Kushinadahime's role as Susanoo's wife signifies the importance of balance and cooperation between masculine and feminine energies.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Kushinadahime continues to be a revered figure in contemporary Japanese culture. Her story is retold in various forms of media, including literature, manga, and anime, ensuring its continued relevance to modern audiences. She is often depicted as a symbol of feminine grace and strength. Many shrines throughout Japan honor her, and festivals celebrate her role in the Yamata no Orochi myth. The Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi sword, found within the serpent, remains one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan, further solidifying Kushinadahime's indirect influence on national identity and heritage.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: The primary source for Kushinadahime's myth, detailing her role in the Yamata no Orochi story.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: An English translation of the Kojiki, providing accessibility to the original text.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Associated with purity and cleansing, reflecting her virtuous nature.

associations.name: Marriage
associations.significance: Her marriage to Susanoo establishes her as a patron of marital happiness.

associations.name: Comb
associations.significance: Represents her transformation and protection during the battle with Yamata no Orochi.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Andromeda
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A princess offered as a sacrifice to a sea monster and rescued by a hero (Perseus).

companions.name: Susanoo
companions.description: The storm god who rescued her from the Yamata no Orochi and became her husband.

companions.name: Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi
companions.description: Her earthly parents, who were grief-stricken by the impending loss of their last daughter.

worshipPractices.name: Shrine Veneration
worshipPractices.description: Worship and offerings at shrines dedicated to Kushinadahime and Susanoo, often seeking blessings for marital harmony and protection.

festivals.name: Various Local Festivals
festivals.description: Local festivals celebrating Kushinadahime and Susanoo, often featuring reenactments of the Yamata no Orochi myth.

cultural.modernLegacy: Kushinadahime's story continues to inspire art, literature, and popular culture, representing themes of sacrifice, protection, and marital bliss within modern interpretations of Japanese mythology.
